title: A resolution expressing the vital importance of the Panama Canal to the United States.
plain_summary: This resolution says that the Panama Canal is very important to the United States for security and trade. The Senate is worried that China is gaining too much control over the canal and the ports around it. They believe China's involvement breaks old treaties that say the canal must stay neutral and not favor any one country. The resolution asks the Trump administration to take steps to make sure the canal remains neutral as promised in those treaties.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations. (text: CR S598)

sections.title: Resolution Expressing the vital importance of the Panama Canal to the United States
sections.text: This resolution states that the Panama Canal is extremely important to the United States. The resolution explains the history of the canal and current concerns about China's involvement. Early attempts by Colombia and French investors to build a canal across Panama failed and went bankrupt by eighteen eighty-nine. When the United States supported Panama's independence from Colombia, including placing American troops in Panama, the United States was promised permanent access to build and control a canal. This agreement was made official in the Hay-Bunau-Varilla Treaty, signed in Washington on November eighteenth, nineteen oh three. The Panamanian government never started, designed, or built the Panama Canal. The United States government paid for, developed, and built the Panama Canal over ten years from nineteen oh four to nineteen fourteen. It cost three hundred seventy-five million dollars and ten thousand lives were lost. The canal was built above sea level using a lock system. Throughout history, the Panama Canal has been separate from Panama's regular territory. The Panama Canal serves as a crucial link between the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans. It connects the east and west coasts of the United States and allowed passage for more than fourteen thousand ships in twenty twenty-three. About seventy-two percent of ships traveling through the Panama Canal are going to or coming from United States ports. Without the Panama Canal, ships would have to pass through the very dangerous Cape Horn, adding nearly eight thousand miles to their trip. In nineteen seventy-seven, President Carter gave up United States control over the Panama Canal through a series of treaties with Panama called the Torrijos-Carter Treaties. One of those treaties, called the Treaty Concerning the Permanent Neutrality and Operation of the Panama Canal or the Neutrality Treaty, was signed in Washington on September seventh, nineteen seventy-seven. This treaty reserved the right of the United States to use military force to defend the permanent neutrality of the Panama Canal. For nearly ten years, the People's Republic of China has steadily increased its presence in the Panama Canal area. In twenty sixteen, Panama gave control of Margarita Island, the Panama Canal's largest Atlantic port, to the China-affiliated Landbridge Group for nine hundred million dollars. In twenty eighteen, Panama made a one billion four hundred million dollar agreement for Chinese companies called China Communications Construction Company and China Harbor Engineering Company to build the fourth bridge across the Panama Canal. A Hong Kong-based company called CK Hutchison Holdings manages two of the Panama Canal's five ports, including the Balboa port on the Pacific side and Cristobal port on the Atlantic side. The rapid growth of Chinese influence in the Panama Canal creates a high risk that China could gather intelligence and conduct surveillance. Chinese law requires civilian companies to make their resources available to support China's military forces. The Panama Canal would serve as a supply point between the east and west coasts of the United States if there was a conflict involving United States Armed Forces, making it very valuable for homeland and regional defense. China's ability to control major entry and exit points of the Panama Canal would give China a significant military advantage against United States Armed Forces if there was a conflict. Therefore, the Senate recognizes the creativity and hard work of Americans that made the Panama Canal possible for future generations, with special respect for those Americans who died working on the Panama Canal project. The Senate states that the Panama Canal is vital to United States regional security, control over the region, and economic interests. The Senate believes that the pattern of Chinese-backed investment in port infrastructure and canal operations in Panama violates the Neutrality Treaty. The Senate urges the Trump administration to ensure that the canal remains neutral and to take all appropriate steps to enforce the Neutrality Treaty.
